Paul begins with a dangerous question: now that grace covers all your sin, why not keep sinning? It seems logical—more sin means more grace, doesn't it? But the question itself reveals a fatal misunderstanding of what happened to you when you became a Christian. You didn't just get forgiven and walk away unchanged. You died. When you were baptized into Christ Jesus, you were baptized into His death—buried with Him so completely that the person you were no longer exists. This isn't poetry or metaphor cushioning hard truth. Paul means that your old identity, your former slavery to sin, ended as definitively as a person in a grave. The God who raised Christ from the dead didn't do so to leave you as you were. He raised Him to new life so you could walk in newness of life, transformed so radically that continuing in sin isn't an upgrade—it's a return to a corpse.

This is where Romans 6 becomes confrontational. You are not neutral ground where sin can do what it wishes. Your body belongs to someone now. You were once slaves to sin, offering your hands, your eyes, your mouth, your desires to wickedness—and what did it profit you? Death, Paul reminds you. Shame. Emptiness. That slavery seemed like freedom once, but it was a master that paid only in destruction. Now you have a choice, though calling it a choice might be generous. You can either present your body as an instrument of unrighteousness or as an instrument of God. There is no third option. There is no neutrality. The parts of your body will serve someone. They will obey someone. They will work toward someone's purposes. Paul demands you see this clearly: you've been emancipated from one slave-master only to become a slave to another. But here's what grace actually is—it's the slave-master who doesn't destroy you, who pays in holiness instead of death, who gives eternal life instead of wages of destruction.

The finality in Paul's closing statement should stop you: "The wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord." Sin pays. Death is what you've earned, what your old service accumulated. But a gift—grace cannot be earned or deserved. It simply comes to you through Christ. You didn't negotiate for it or negotiate down from it. Your resurrection with Him wasn't negotiable either. If you've died with Christ, you will live with Him. So the question returns to you now, stripped of its false logic. You've been set free from sin. That freedom was purchased with Christ's blood and secured in His resurrection. The only question left isn't whether you can sin—you can, and you will stumble. The question is whether you will present yourself to your true Master, whether you will count yourself dead to sin's demands and alive to God's purposes. That's not burdensome obedience to law. That's the reasonable response to having been loved at the cost of everything.
